title: Why Linear Regression Still Matters in the Age of LLMs
author: Rohit Ghumare
excerpt: Before you reach for a billion-parameter model, consider what a single weighted sum can tell you about your data.
content:
The Quiet Workhorse

Every week brings a new LLM benchmark, a new reasoning model, a new agent framework. In that noise, it's easy to forget that linear regression — the humble y = mx + b extended to many dimensions — is still one of the most deployed models in production across industries.

Why? Because it is interpretable, fast to train, requires minimal data, and often performs within a few percentage points of far more complex models on tabular tasks.

When Complexity Hurts

Not every problem needs a transformer. In fact, for many business problems, adding complexity makes things worse:

  • Overfitting — Complex models memorize noise in small datasets.
  • Latency — Serving an LLM for every prediction is expensive and slow.
  • Interpretability — Stakeholders need to know why a prediction was made, not just what it was.
  • Maintenance — Simpler models have fewer failure modes.

The Baseline Test

Here is a rule every ML team should enforce: no fancy model until a linear baseline exists. Train a linear or logistic regression first. Log its performance. Only move to more complex architectures if the gap justifies the added cost.

This isn't about being retro. It's about being rigorous. When your random forest or neural net only beats linear regression by 2%, you have to ask: is that worth the extra compute, the longer training time, and the opaque predictions?

Modern Enhancements

Linear regression has not stood still. Modern variants include:

  • Ridge and Lasso — Add regularization to handle multicollinearity and feature selection automatically.
  • Bayesian linear regression — Output distributions instead of point estimates, giving you uncertainty quantification for free.
  • Generalized Additive Models (GAMs) — Keep the linear structure but learn non-linear feature transformations.

Conclusion

The best engineers know when to reach for the simple tool. Linear regression is not a stepping stone — it is a destination for a huge class of problems. Treat it with the respect it deserves, and your systems will be faster, cheaper, and easier to explain.
